title: What are weak references, and how does WeakHashMap differ from HashMap in terms of garbage collection?

answers.junior: A weak reference in Java is a reference type that does not prevent its referent from being garbage collected. Java provides four reference strengths — strong, soft, weak, and phantom — defined in the java.lang.ref package.
answers.middle: A weak reference in Java is a reference type that does not prevent its referent from being garbage collected. Java provides four reference strengths — strong, soft, weak, and phantom — defined in the java.lang.ref package. A WeakReference<T> object wraps another object, and if the only references pointing to that object are weak (no strong or soft references exist), the GC is free to collect it during the next collection cycle, regardless of available heap space. You create one like: WeakReference<MyObject> ref = new WeakReference<>(new MyObject()); and retrieve the referent via ref.get(), which returns null once the object has been collected. WeakHashMap leverages this mechanism by storing its keys as weak references internally.
answers.senior: A weak reference in Java is a reference type that does not prevent its referent from being garbage collected. Java provides four reference strengths — strong, soft, weak, and phantom — defined in the java.lang.ref package. A WeakReference<T> object wraps another object, and if the only references pointing to that object are weak (no strong or soft references exist), the GC is free to collect it during the next collection cycle, regardless of available heap space. You create one like: WeakReference<MyObject> ref = new WeakReference<>(new MyObject()); and retrieve the referent via ref.get(), which returns null once the object has been collected. WeakHashMap leverages this mechanism by storing its keys as weak references internally. In a standard HashMap, both keys and values are strongly referenced, so entries persist as long as the map itself is alive. In a WeakHashMap, when a key object becomes weakly reachable (no strong references to it exist outside the map), the GC can collect that key, and the corresponding entry is automatically removed from the map via a ReferenceQueue that WeakHashMap polls on structural operations. This makes WeakHashMap ideal for caches and canonicalized-mapping scenarios where you want entries to be evicted automatically when keys are no longer in use elsewhere, avoiding memory leaks: WeakHashMap<Widget, CachedData> cache = new WeakHashMap<>();. There are important caveats to understand in production use.
answers.tricky: A weak reference in Java is a reference type that does not prevent its referent from being garbage collected. Java provides four reference strengths — strong, soft, weak, and phantom — defined in the java.lang.ref package. A WeakReference<T> object wraps another object, and if the only references pointing to that object are weak (no strong or soft references exist), the GC is free to collect it during the next collection cycle, regardless of available heap space. You create one like: WeakReference<MyObject> ref = new WeakReference<>(new MyObject()); and retrieve the referent via ref.get(), which returns null once the object has been collected. WeakHashMap leverages this mechanism by storing its keys as weak references internally. In a standard HashMap, both keys and values are strongly referenced, so entries persist as long as the map itself is alive. In a WeakHashMap, when a key object becomes weakly reachable (no strong references to it exist outside the map), the GC can collect that key, and the corresponding entry is automatically removed from the map via a ReferenceQueue that WeakHashMap polls on structural operations. This makes WeakHashMap ideal for caches and canonicalized-mapping scenarios where you want entries to be evicted automatically when keys are no longer in use elsewhere, avoiding memory leaks: WeakHashMap<Widget, CachedData> cache = new WeakHashMap<>();. There are important caveats to understand in production use. Primitive-boxed keys like Integer can be problematic if the JVM caches the boxed instance (e.g., values -128 to 127 are cached by Integer.valueOf, so those keys may never be collected). String literals interned in the string pool are strongly referenced by the JVM and will never be collected either, making them poor WeakHashMap keys. WeakHashMap is also not thread-safe; if concurrent access is needed, you must wrap it with Collections.synchronizedMap or use a purpose-built solution like Guava's CacheBuilder with weak keys. Finally, values in WeakHashMap are still stored as strong references, so if a value holds a strong reference back to its key, the key will never be collected, creating an unintentional memory leak — a common and subtle pitfall to watch for in production code.
